title: From Zero to MVP in Two Days: Building a Family Calendar with AI Agents
summary: I threw "build a family calendar" at my OPC loop, and two days later had a full Next.js app supporting image/voice/text input, LLM parsing, and family access controls running on my screen. This is the real-time record of that process—including where the AI went off track, and how I yanked it back on course.
body:
## The One-Sentence Spec

"I want to build a family calendar feature that only our family members can see (requires login), with the ability to upload images, voice input, text input—have an LLM process all that, confirm it, then record it as our family calendar, plus email and other notification channels to remind us."

That was it. Then I tossed it to `/opc loop`.

This isn't groundbreaking stuff. But two days later, staring at a working app on my screen, I had a feeling worth writing down: this actually happened, and the pace was way faster than I expected, while the pitfalls were way deeper than I imagined.

---

## What Is OPC Loop

OPC (One Person Company) is an AI-driven development workflow I maintain. The core principle boils down to one sentence: **the agent that implements something can't be the one evaluating it**.

Each functional unit follows three steps:
1. implementer subagent builds it
2. independent reviewer (tester + domain role) evaluates it  
3. gate decision: PASS moves forward, FAIL sends it back for rework

`/opc loop` is the batch mode—give it a big task, it auto-decomposes into units, queues them up, runs each unit through the full implement-review-gate cycle, can run unattended for 24 hours.

For this family calendar project, OPC broke the requirements into roughly 11 units, from database schema to auth to frontend pages to push notifications, lined up in sequence.

---

## The Real Rhythm

**Day 1 Morning**: OPC started building the skeleton. Next.js 14 + Vercel Postgres + NextAuth. AI knows this stack well—ran smoothly. Schema design, API routes, auth middleware, barely needed me to intervene.

I asked one question: "Do we need passwords? Can't we do real verification?"

Behind this was a real product decision: how should family member auth work? Passwords are too annoying (family members won't remember passwords), Magic Links are elegant but depend on email config, Google OAuth is the most frictionless.

OPC chose Google OAuth + NextAuth, I confirmed, it continued.

**Day 1 Afternoon**: The LLM parsing layer emerged. This is the product's core feature—users upload an image (like a kid's school notice), or say something ("pick up the kid next Wednesday at 3pm"), and the LLM parses it into structured calendar events.

Here AI made a natural choice: use Anthropic Claude's multimodal API, throw base64 images and text prompts at it together, have it output `{ title, startAt, endAt, location, description }` JSON.

But I wasn't satisfied. My `~/.claude/.env` has three model groups—Claude, OpenAI GPT-4o, and a domestic model. I wanted them as mutual fallbacks, forming an LLM pool where one provider going down wouldn't break functionality.

OPC implemented `src/lib/llm/pool.ts`, trying by priority order, returning on first success. This design proved invaluable later, since Claude API does occasionally 503.

**Day 1 Evening**: AI delivered the first frontend version. Then I opened the page and frowned.

It built a standard calendar grid—month view, with date cells, events displayed in cells. Functionally fine, but **this wasn't what I wanted**.

My family members aren't Google Calendar power users. They don't need a grid, they need a "what's happening today" "what's coming up" "Agent remind me" interface—more like a feed than a spreadsheet.

---

## The Most Important Interruption

This was the most critical intervention of the entire session.

`/opc loop` was already halfway through, deliverable was sitting there. But the direction was wrong. I didn't let it continue optimizing in the wrong direction—I interrupted and re-described the product shape:

"This shouldn't be calendar-main-interface focused—the main interface should be more human friendly, easy to add new stuff, easy to see previous stuff, more about the agent doing reminders, redo it."

This one sentence cost us the already-built month view component and meant redesigning the homepage. Worth it? Absolutely.

New homepage design:
- **HeroHeader**: Welcome message + today's event count + pending Agent suggestions count
- **AgentCardList**: AI-generated suggestion cards, one-click confirm to become calendar events
- **TodayTaskList**: Today's agenda, timeline style
- **UpcomingChips**: Next 7 days' events as small chips
- **FabBar**: Bottom floating input bar, add anytime

This structure fits family use cases better than month view. AI handles input, humans read feeds, Agent does reminders—clean separation of responsibilities.

---

## A Small Auth Gotcha

Local development used `STUB_AUTH=true` environment variable, making auth middleware just pass through. This is reasonable DX design, but created a problem: E2E tests trying to test auth-protected behavior couldn't actually test real 401s locally.

Solution was explicitly commenting in E2E specs "STUB_AUTH=true means POST/DELETE auth check passes locally", then adjusting test strategy to: test validation (400s), don't test auth flow (401s only matter in real environments).

This decision isn't perfect, but it's practical.

---

## The Numbers

After two days, codebase status:
- **11 OPC units** all complete
- **6 E2E spec files**, 52 tests, 0 failures
- **LLM pool** supporting 3 providers with auto-fallback
- **Push notifications** (Web Push + VAPID) integrated
- **PWA manifest** and service worker in place
- TypeScript zero errors

This pace would have been impossible two years ago. Even one year ago was hard to imagine—not technically impossible, but "using AI to drive entire development workflows" needed a framework to contain it, otherwise AI would drift off course or get stuck fixing the same bug forever.

OPC loop solves this problem: it doesn't make AI smarter, it makes AI's work structured.

---

## Reflection: Where AI Most Easily Goes Off Track

**1. Initial design choices**. AI has a strong "finish a working version first" bias. This often leads it to pick designs that are "functionally correct but directionally wrong." The month view was exactly this—nobody told it "don't build a grid," so it built a grid, because that's the most common calendar UI.

Product direction decisions must be human-driven. AI can execute, but it doesn't have your user personas.

**2. Auth and environment variable assumptions**. AI introduces stubs when designing local dev experience, which is good. But sometimes it doesn't proactively tell you "this stub will affect certain test validity." You need to think through that layer yourself.

**3. Test tautology problems**. AI writing tests very easily falls into "testing mocks it wrote"—page.route() mocks an API, then asserts content from the mock. That's not testing, that's a parrot. This issue got pulled out and fixed separately in later E2E-focused work.

---

Two days, one family calendar from zero to MVP. This article is a process record, not methodology evangelism. If you want to know what AI-driven development actually feels like—sometimes you're watching the terminal waiting for it to finish, sometimes you're staring at the screen deciding whether to interrupt it, sometimes it delivers solutions you never thought of that turn out better than your original ideas—this is roughly what it's like.
